---
title: "Where Quasi-Monte Carlo Theory and Practice Meet"
author: "Fred J. Hickernell"
subtitle: |
  Illinois Institute of Technology, hickernell@illinoistech.edu

  Joint work with several collaborators and the <span class="pkg">qmcpy</span> development team

  Supported in part by NSF-DMS-2316011 &nbsp; &nbsp;  ·  &nbsp; &nbsp; Thanks to the organizers

  Monte Carlo and Quasi-Monte Carlo Methods 2026 (MCQMC26), Edinburgh

  🔗 Slides: fjhickernell.github.io/MCQMC26/slides/MCQMC26.html

date: 2026-06-08
date-format: "MMMM D, YYYY"

metadata-files:
  - ../classlib/classlib/quarto/slides/research/research-slides.yml

execute:
  echo: false

format:
  revealjs:
    code-overflow: wrap
    footer: |
      Fred J. Hickernell · Illinois Institute of Technology · assisted by ChatGPT

---


# Quasi-Monte Carlo (QMC) 

\begin{equation*}
\overbrace{\Ex[f(\vX)] = \int_{[0,1]^d} f(\vx) \, \dif \vx}^{\alerttextsf{population mean}} 
\approx \underbrace{\frac 1n \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} f(\vx_i)}_{\alerttextsf{sample mean}}, \qquad \text{where }\vX \sim \Unif([0,1]^d)
\end{equation*}


Want 

- Deterministic or probabilistic [estimate/bound]{.alert} on error: $\displaystyle\Biggl \lvert \int_{[0,1]^d} f(\vx) \, \dif \vx - \frac 1n \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} f(\vx_i) \Biggr \rvert$

- [Economical computational cost]{.alert}: $\Order \bigl( n \, \$(f) + n^p \bigr)$ 
  - $\$(f)$ is cost of evaluating $f$ once
  - $\Order(n^p)$ is the cost of the error estimate/bound




## QMC Has Grown Over Several Decades

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="35%"}
\begin{multline*}
\overbrace{\Ex[f(\vX)] = \int_{[0,1]^d} f(\vx) \, \dif \vx}^{\alerttextsf{population mean}} \\
\approx \underbrace{\frac 1n \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} f(\vx_i)}_{\alerttextsf{sample mean}}
\end{multline*}

* [Practice]{.alert}
  - Motivates and illustrates theory 
  - Solves real problems

* [Theory]{.alert} justifies and explains practice


* [Foundation]{.alert} enables theory


:::

::: {.column .tree-col width="65%"}

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis

import runpy

helper = runpy.run_path(
    "../classlib/classlib/quarto/assets/images/qmc-tree/render_qmc_tree.py"
)

helper["render_qmc_tree"](
    show_group_labels=True,
)
```

:::

:::




## No QMC with roots only

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="35%"}

\begin{multline*}
\overbrace{\Ex[f(\vX)] = \int_{[0,1]^d} f(\vx) \, \dif \vx}^{\alerttextsf{population mean}} \\
\approx \underbrace{\frac 1n \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} f(\vx_i)}_{\alerttextsf{sample mean}}
\end{multline*}

&nbsp;

&nbsp;

&nbsp;

* [Foundation]{.alert} enables theory

  - Rooted in multiple disciplines 
  - Rare for a single person to have expertise in all of them

:::

::: {.column .tree-col width="65%"}

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis

import runpy

helper = runpy.run_path(
    "../classlib/classlib/quarto/assets/images/qmc-tree/render_qmc_tree.py"
)

helper["render_qmc_tree"](
    groups=["foundation"],
    group_labels=["foundation"],
    mask="root",
)
```

:::

:::

## A strong trunk supports the canopy

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="35%"}

\begin{multline*}
\overbrace{\Ex[f(\vX)] = \int_{[0,1]^d} f(\vx) \, \dif \vx}^{\alerttextsf{population mean}} \\
\approx \underbrace{\frac 1n \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} f(\vx_i)}_{\alerttextsf{sample mean}}
\end{multline*}


&nbsp;

* [Theory]{.alert} 

  - Draws on the foundation
  
  - Explains what may and may not work

  - Grows in response to suprises in practice

:::

::: {.column .tree-col width="65%"}

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis

import runpy

helper = runpy.run_path(
    "../classlib/classlib/quarto/assets/images/qmc-tree/render_qmc_tree.py"
)

helper["render_qmc_tree"](
    groups=["theory"],
    group_labels=["theory"],
    mask="canopy",
)
```

:::

:::


## Canopy is the most visible

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="35%"}

* [Practice]{.alert}
  - Solving problems with societal impact
  - Infrastructure for practitioners
  - Requires theory for justification
  - May not seem to align with theory at first

:::

::: {.column .tree-col width="65%"}

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis

import runpy

helper = runpy.run_path(
    "../classlib/classlib/quarto/assets/images/qmc-tree/render_qmc_tree.py"
)

helper["render_qmc_tree"](
    groups=["practice"],
    group_labels=["practice"],
)
```

:::

:::


# Low Discrepancy Sequence Constructions

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="65%"}

\begin{multline*}
\left\lvert \int_{[0,1]^d} f(\vx) \, \dif \vx - \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} f(\vx_i) \right\rvert
\\ \le \underbracket{\Dsc\bigl(\{\vx_i\}_{i=0}^{n-1}\bigr)}_{\substack{\alerttextsf{Discrepancy} \\ \Order(\alert{n^{-1+\delta}}) \textsf{ or } \alerttextsf{better}}} \, \lVert f \rVert_{\cf} \ \mathlink{https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-3-032-10590-5_1}{\textsf{HKS26}}
\end{multline*}
<!-- \yamlref{hickirsor_2026_qmc_tutorial} -->

::: {.fragment}

Careful choices of $\vx_0, \vx_1, \ldots$ lead to low discrepancy:

- [Digital sequences]{.alert} [{{< meta papers.dicpil_2010_digital_nets_sequences.cite >}}]({{< meta papers.dicpil_2010_digital_nets_sequences.publisher_url >}})
- [Lattice sequences]{.alert} [{{< meta papers.dickripil_2022_lattice_rules_book.cite >}}]({{< meta papers.dickripil_2022_lattice_rules_book.doi_url >}})
- [Halton]{.alert} [{{< meta papers.halton_1960_sequences.cite >}}]({{< meta papers.halton_1960_sequences.publisher_url >}})
- [Kronecker sequences]{.alert} [{{< meta papers.richtmyer_1951_sequences.cite >}}]({{< meta papers.richtmyer_1951_sequences.publisher_url >}})
- [Optimization and neural network based]{.alert} [{{< meta papers.fangmawin_2002_centered_l2.cite >}}]({{< meta papers.fangmawin_2002_centered_l2.publisher_url >}}), [{{< meta papers.ruskirbrolemrus_2024_mpmc.cite >}}]({{< meta papers.ruskirbrolemrus_2024_mpmc.doi_url >}}) 

:::

:::


::: {.column .tree-col width="35%"}

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis

import runpy

helper = runpy.run_path(
    "../classlib/classlib/quarto/assets/images/qmc-tree/render_qmc_tree.py"
)

helper["render_qmc_tree"](
    labels=["sequence", "discrepancy", "missing", "random"],
    tree_font_scale=1.5,
)
```

::: {.fragment}

- Extensible sequences facilitate adaptive error control

- Number theory or component-by-component (CBC) constructions [{{< meta papers.cookuonuy_2006_constructing_embedded.cite >}}]({{< meta papers.cookuonuy_2006_constructing_embedded.publisher_url >}})

- Digital and lattice sequences have preferred $n = 1, b, b^2, \ldots$

- Randomization removes bias and points on the boundary

:::

:::

:::

## Missing data by design or by accident

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="65%"}

### Problem

- Some QMC software intentionally

  - Omits $\vzero = (0,0,\ldots)$ because it is on the boundary

  - Facilitates skipping points or starting at a later point in the sequence

- $f(\vx_i)$ might be NaN or $\infty$ for some $i$?

- Breaks the net or lattice structure

::: {.fragment}

### Solution 1

Persuade the software developers to 

- Not omit points 

- Randomize to get them off the boundary

:::

:::

::: {.column .tree-col width="35%"}
```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis

import runpy

helper = runpy.run_path(
    "../classlib/classlib/quarto/assets/images/qmc-tree/render_qmc_tree.py"
)

helper["render_qmc_tree"](
    labels=["sequence", "discrepancy", "missing", "random", "software"],
    tree_font_scale=1.5,
)
```
:::{.fragment}

### Solution 2

- If time allows, use optimal sample weights with the data that you have

- Construct sequences that are good for all $n$

- Be happy with $\Order(n^{-1+\delta})$ convergence

:::

:::

:::


## Sequences that are good for all $n$



# Data-Driven Error Bounds

# Software Development and Dissemination

<!-- comment out for now, but will add back in later

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis
#| eval: false

import runpy

helper = runpy.run_path("../qmcsoftware/scripts/qmc_software_table.py")

helper["render_qmc_software_table"](
    "../qmcsoftware/data/qmc-software.yml",
    mode="slides",
    start=0,
    stop=10,
)
```

---

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis
#| eval: false
import runpy

import runpy

helper = runpy.run_path("../qmcsoftware/scripts/qmc_software_table.py")

helper["render_qmc_software_table"](
    "../qmcsoftware/data/qmc-software.yml",
    mode="slides",
    start=10,
    stop=20,
)

```
-->

# Collaboration and Community

## Thanks to many collaborators

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis

import yaml
from pathlib import Path
people = yaml.safe_load(Path("../data/collaborators.yml").read_text())
ncols = 6
img_width = "135px"
print(f'<div class="collab-grid" style="grid-template-columns: repeat({ncols}, 1fr);">')
for person in people:
    slug = person["slug"]
    caption = person["caption"]
    img = f"../assets/img/headshots/processed/{slug}.jpg"
    print(f'''
<div class="collab-card">
  <img src="{img}" alt="{caption}" style="width:{img_width};">
  <div class="collab-caption">{caption}</div>
</div>
''')

print("</div>")
```
---

# Future Directions

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="35%"}

* [Practice]{.alert}
  - Solving problems with societal impact
  - Infrastructure for practitioners
  - Requires theory for justification
  - May not seem to align with theory at first

:::

::: {.column .tree-col width="65%"}

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis

import runpy

helper = runpy.run_path(
    "../classlib/classlib/quarto/assets/images/qmc-tree/render_qmc_tree.py"
)

helper["render_qmc_tree"](
    groups=["foundation", "theory", "practice"],
    group_labels=["foundation", "theory", "practice"],
)
```

:::

:::


# References {#references}

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis

import sys
from pathlib import Path

sys.path.insert(0, str(Path("../classlib").resolve()))

from classlib.quarto.references.render_references import render_reference_slides

render_reference_slides(
    deck_path="Plenary_MCQMC2026.qmd",
    refs_path="../classlib/classlib/quarto/metadata/hickernell-papers.yml",
    max_lines=36,
    wrap_width=92,
)
```
